---
title: "A3 - Containerization with Docker"
output: html_document
---

# A3 - Containerization with Docker

## Useful References
- [Docker Curriculum](https://docker-curriculum.com/)
- [Rocker Project (Bioconductor Containers)](https://rocker-project.org/)

## Learning Objectives
In this session we will cover:
- Why packages and OS versions break code over time.
- What a Docker container is.
- How to build a custom `Dockerfile` containing your R environment.

## The Reproducibility Crisis in Bioinformatics
You publish a paper. Two years later, a reviewer asks to rerun the script. Oh no! 
Your Mac updated to a new OS, R is now version 4.5, and `Seurat` updated, completely breaking your code. 

**Docker** solves this by freezing the OS, the R version, and all packages into an immutable "container".

## The Dockerfile
A `Dockerfile` is a plain text blueprint. Here is an example of what it looks like for an R Data Science project:

```dockerfile
# 1. Start from an existing blueprint containing R 4.2.0 and the Tidyverse
FROM rocker/tidyverse:4.2.0

# 2. Install any extra Linux dependencies (e.g. for building packages)
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y libcurl4-openssl-dev

# 3. Install Bioconductor inside the container
RUN R -e "install.packages('BiocManager'); BiocManager::install('DESeq2')"

# 4. Copy your local scripts into the container
COPY ./analysis_script.R /app/analysis_script.R

# 5. Tell the container what to do when it runs
CMD ["Rscript", "/app/analysis_script.R"]
```

## Building and Running
In your terminal, you build the image and run it. It behaves exactly the same on a Windows laptop or a massive Linux server.

```{bash building-docker, eval=FALSE}
# Build the actual container from the blueprint
# (Giving it the tag 'my-bio-analysis')
docker build -t my-bio-analysis .

# Run the container!
docker run --rm my-bio-analysis
```
